explanation.text: Alan calmly calculates the air, battery time, distance, and physical limits of several escape plans before adapting the nitrogen cylinder. This methodical problem-solving shows that he stays focused even though a mistake could kill him.

Comparing the tools to brittle glass emphasizes that Titan's extreme cold has made them likely to snap. This detail increases the danger because Alan must remove stubborn bolts without breaking the tools his escape depends on.

The high-pressure nitrogen blasts the cylinder upward and pulls Alan high into the air, giving him momentum before he opens his wings. This improvised launch allows him to glide farther and faster toward the base before his life-support resources fail.

Alan deliberately controls his fear even as he loses altitude and his survival depends on continued flight. His recognition that panic would cause disaster most clearly reveals his self-control under extreme pressure.

The "massive appendages" beat as Alan flaps and produce lift, so the phrase refers to his mechanical wings. Because an appendage extends from or is attached to something, the word fits the wings connected to his suit.

The narration closely follows Alan's calculations, physical pain, fear, equipment readings, and uncertain progress toward the base. This limited access explains why his dangerous moment-by-moment experience makes the story suspenseful.

Alan cannot survive the trip by walking or flying under his own power, so he turns the nitrogen cylinder into a rocket-like launch. Its blast gives him the extra speed and distance central to his escape, making that event essential to a summary.

New York continues to use the Hudson as a sewer but no longer values it as the source that helped build the city. This contrast indicates that the river is losing importance in people's minds, not physically disappearing.

The article asserts that river industries made some New Yorkers rich but gives no names, amounts, or examples to demonstrate that wealth. By contrast, specific wastes support the pollution claims and the later court case supports the environmental dreamers' actions.

Paragraph 7 predicts that industrial and environmental dreamers will meet in court. This sentence connects the history of pollution with the following conflict between Con Edison and Scenic Hudson, transitioning to the changes produced by activism.
Con Edison abandons its plan to carve a huge hole into Storm King Mountain, so the mountain survives without the proposed damage. In this context, "unblemished" means unharmed.

The statement says the river can continue supporting people only if people care for the river in return. It therefore expresses the article's idea that those who benefit from a natural resource also have a duty to protect it.

The pursuit of industrial wealth leads people to dump sewage, chemicals, factory waste, and garbage into the Hudson until fishing collapses. This cause-and-effect relationship is central to the article and therefore belongs in its summary.

The author personally accepts responsibility for helping keep the river healthy for future dreamers. This forward-looking commitment most clearly reveals his view that protecting the Hudson is a shared, continuing obligation.

The restaurant kitchen is full of clattering sound, bright light, motion, and constant orders, and it is the center of the family's life. Calling it a "nerve center" therefore suggests both energetic activity and a central role.

The narrator's early decision that restaurant work is what she wants to do guides her frustration with school and her detailed plans for becoming a chef. This statement shows her passion for a culinary career and best expresses the story's central idea.

Julia Child begins cooking near age forty but goes on to create television shows and cookbooks and become important in culinary history. Her example shows that she successfully pursued a career that deeply interested her.

The narrator is sure she will be discovered but can describe the process only as "somehow." That vague word reveals that her goal is firm even though she has not worked out every step for reaching it.

To "pay my dues" means the narrator expects to put in time and effort before becoming a celebrity chef. This acknowledgment shows that she understands success will require more than simply naming her goal.

The narrator's admiration for Julia Child and other television chefs shapes her dream of having a cooking show, books, products, and fans of her own. That aspiration is central to the story, while the other choices are minor background details.

Most of the story consists of the narrator's private reactions to homework, judgments about school, memories of restaurant work, and imagined future as a chef. This internal monologue reveals and develops her ambitious, confident point of view.

The article describes social criticism, restrictive expectations, ridicule, and fear of equality as obstacles facing women cyclists. The "Few Flat Tires" metaphor shows that women encountered resistance, rather than only literal bicycle trouble, along their path toward freedom.
The word "enwrapped" shows that Willard was enclosed in long skirts, which could restrict the movement of her legs. That physical constraint helps explain that "impeded" means hindered or slowed.

Before this sentence, women of different classes are confined, anonymous, or absent from public life; afterward, the author describes women cycling and becoming visible. This shift shows that "But the bicycle changed that" marks the transition between those contrasting conditions.

Wealthier women face restrictive clothing and domestic confinement, while poorer women labor anonymously in mills and factories. By saying the result was the same, the author indicates that women across economic classes were treated unfairly and excluded from public prominence.

The magazines describe cycling as creating independence, confidence, delight, and a sense of new power, even comparing women riders to people who have grown wings. These effects show that bicycles offered personal and social liberation beyond the ability to travel farther.

The article says there is no evidence Stanton ever rode a bicycle, even though she strongly advocated for it. Willard, however, wrote a best-selling account of learning to ride at age fifty-three, so actual riding distinguishes her from Stanton.

Bicycles bring women out of their homes, make female athletes and models publicly visible, and become linked to demands for greater rights. This evidence shows why the statement that women were starting to be seen and heard best captures the article's broad transformation.
